perturbation.original_text: 1.4) "Evaluation" means an evaluation by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's Samples for the purpose of testing against those methods as specified in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. All tests available reside in the Petro-Lube Testing Brochure or on the Petro-Lube web site or by quotation.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4) "Evaluation" means an evaluation by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's Samples for the purpose of testing against methods chosen at the Receiving Party's sole discretion, regardless of any specification in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. All tests available reside in the Petro-Lube Testing Brochure or on the Petro-Lube web site or by quotation.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction. Originally, the Evaluation was tied to the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order specifications. Now, the Receiving Party (Petro-Lube) can choose methods regardless of those specifications, creating uncertainty and potentially invalidating the agreement's intent. This directly contradicts the implications present in section 3.1 and 3.2.3 where the Receiving party is supposed to perform evaluations as directed by the Disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1) Subject to Clauses 3.2 and 3.3, Petro-Lube is authorized to perform Evaluations on (Your Company Name) Samples.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the 'Evaluation' definition, granting the Receiving Party sole discretion in choosing test methods, regardless of the Disclosing Party's specifications. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which authorizes Petro-Lube to perform evaluations on (Your Company Name) Samples. The original text tied evaluation to the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order, maintaining consistency, while the changed text introduces a conflicting obligation. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2.3) Not to use any Sample for any purpose other than to perform those Evaluations as directed by (Your Company Name).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2.3) Not to use any Sample for any purpose other than to perform those Evaluations, unless Petro-Lube deems another evaluation necessary for comprehensive analysis.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation modifies the original clause that restricted Petro-Lube from using samples for purposes other than those directed by (Your Company Name). By adding the exception allowing Petro-Lube to decide on additional evaluations, a direct contradiction is introduced because it goes against the specifications and consent required from the disclosing party. The contradiction impacts clause 1.4, where the meaning of evaluation is defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.4) "Evaluation" means an evaluation by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's Samples for the purpose of testing against those methods as specified in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. All tests available reside in the Petro-Lube Testing Brochure or on the Petro-Lube web site or by quotation.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the sample can only be used for evaluations directed by (Your Company Name). The changed text introduces an exception, allowing Petro-Lube to decide on additional evaluations. This directly contradicts clause 1.4, which defines 'Evaluation' as testing against methods specified in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. The change allows Petro-Lube to conduct evaluations outside of those specified in the Purchase Order, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4) At the conclusion of the Evaluation Petro-Lube shall either return promptly to (Your Company Name) or it's Assigns, or at (Your Company Name)'s option, destroy any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control and verify such to (Your Company Name).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4) At the conclusion of the Evaluation, unless further evaluations are pending, Petro-Lube shall either return promptly to (Your Company Name) or it's Assigns, or at (Your Company Name)'s option, destroy any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control and verify such to (Your Company Name). Petro-Lube may retain a portion of the sample for archival purposes or further internal research, at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding the disposal of samples. The original text states Petro-Lube must return or destroy the sample after evaluation. The addition allows Petro-Lube to retain a portion for archival or research, contradicting the original obligation. This conflicts with Section 3.4's initial instruction on concluding the evaluation, making it unclear when the sample needs to be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4) At the conclusion of the Evaluation Petro-Lube shall either return promptly to (Your Company Name) or it's Assigns, or at (Your Company Name)'s option, destroy any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control and verify such to (Your Company Name).
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that Petro-Lube either return or destroy the sample after evaluation. The changed text adds a provision allowing Petro-Lube to retain a portion of the sample for archival or research purposes. This directly contradicts the original text's requirement for the sample's disposal, creating an inconsistency as it's now unclear whether Petro-Lube must return/destroy the entire sample. The contradicted text is the original language from section 3.4 which is in direct conflict with the changed version, as per the provided explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1) It shall not be necessary for either Party to disclose to the other Party any confidential technical or commercial data or information about its products, technology, processes, commercial activities or otherwise for the purposes of this Agreement. To the extent that either Party does disclose such data or information to the other Party, the other Party shall be entitled to regard such information as non-confidential in the absence of a written agreement between the Parties otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1) It shall not be necessary for either Party to disclose to the other Party any confidential technical or commercial data or information about its products, technology, processes, commercial activities or otherwise for the purposes of this Agreement, except when Petro-Lube deems it necessary for comprehensive evaluation. To the extent that either Party does disclose such data or information to the other Party, the other Party shall be entitled to regard such information as non-confidential in the absence of a written agreement between the Parties otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation creates an exception to the non-disclosure clause, allowing Petro-Lube to demand confidential information if deemed necessary for evaluation. This directly contradicts the initial statement that disclosure is unnecessary. It introduces uncertainty as to what constitutes 'necessary' disclosure and undermines the original intent of Section 4 to protect confidential information. This creates an in-text contradiction within section 4.1, since the start says its not neccessary to disclose any information, while the other party can deem something neccessary for comprehensive evaluation. The original texts also states in the end that information is deemed non-confidential without a written agreement, but with the inclusion of Petro-Lube to deem something neccessary, this creates an in-text contradicion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1) It shall not be necessary for either Party to disclose to the other Party any confidential technical or commercial data or information about its products, technology, processes, commercial activities or otherwise for the purposes of this Agreement. To the extent that either Party does disclose such data or information to the other Party, the other Party shall be entitled to regard such information as non-confidential in the absence of a written agreement between the Parties otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the non-disclosure clause, allowing Petro-Lube to demand confidential information if deemed necessary for evaluation. This directly contradicts the initial statement that disclosure is unnecessary. The original text in section 4.1 states that 'It shall not be necessary for either Party to disclose' any confidential information, while the changed text adds 'except when Petro-Lube deems it necessary for comprehensive evaluation.' This creates an in-text contradiction within section 4.1, as well as to the contradicted text, since the start says its not necessary to disclose any information, while the other party can deem something necessary for comprehensive evaluation. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction, where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties'.

perturbation.original_text: 7.4) The Evaluation and supply of Samples will commence as soon as is practicable after the Effective Date. This Agreement will last for one year from the Effective Date. The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date. This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving thirty (30) days prior written notification. This Agreement may be extended by mutual agreement and written consent of both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.4) The Evaluation and supply of Samples will commence as soon as is practicable after the Effective Date. This Agreement will last for one year from the Effective Date, unless Petro-Lube requires an extended evaluation period. The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date. This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving thirty (30) days prior written notification, unless significant evaluations are still pending. This Agreement may be extended by mutual agreement and written consent of both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause adds exceptions to the one-year duration and the termination clause based on Petro-Lube's needs (extended evaluation period, pending evaluations). This creates a conflict because it allows Petro-Lube to unilaterally extend the agreement or delay termination, contradicting the original terms. This introduces uncertainty and potential for dispute and creating a contradiction to the whole section itself. This introduces an in-text contradiction since this undermines mutual agreement as the original texts specify.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4) The Evaluation and supply of Samples will commence as soon as is practicable after the Effective Date. This Agreement will last for one year from the Effective Date. The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date. This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving thirty (30) days prior written notification. This Agreement may be extended by mutual agreement and written consent of both Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces exceptions to the one-year duration and termination clause based on Petro-Lube's needs, allowing them to unilaterally extend the agreement or delay termination. This directly contradicts the original terms of a fixed one-year duration and termination at any time with 30-day notice, creating an inconsistency and uncertainty within the contract. This fits the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' because it provides conflicting obligations within the same section of the contract. The contradicted text reaffirms the original terms, highlighting the direct conflict introduced by the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: 7.7) No amendment, modification or waiver of any term of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by each of the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.7) No amendment, modification or waiver of any term of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by each of the Parties, except in cases where Petro-Lube determines that a modification is necessary to ensure accurate and reliable test results.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an exception to the amendment clause, allowing Petro-Lube to unilaterally modify the agreement if it deems it necessary for test results. This contradicts the original requirement for mutual written agreement. By granting Petro-Lube unilateral power, this change creates legal uncertainty and a direct in-text contradiction to the initial premise of mutual agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.7) No amendment, modification or waiver of any term of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by each of the Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 7.7 to allow Petro-Lube to unilaterally amend the agreement in certain circumstances. This directly contradicts the original clause 7.7, which states that any amendment requires written agreement from all parties. The contradiction is clear because the original clause requires mutual agreement, while the modified clause allows for unilateral changes by Petro-Lube under specific conditions, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
